name: Ethan Morales-Chen
chief_complaint: My chest just suddenly started hurting really bad on the left side and I can't take a deep breath
onset_description: Was sitting in his dorm room playing video games when he felt a sudden sharp pop in his left chest followed by immediate pain and difficulty breathing. No trauma, no heavy lifting. Symptoms started about 45 minutes ago and are not improving.

acute_chest_pain.description: It felt like something popped in my chest and then it just started killing me on the left side
acute_chest_pain.character: sharp, stabbing, constant with pleuritic component

dyspnea.description: I can't take a full breath, it's like my left lung just stopped working or something
dyspnea.onset: 45 minutes ago, immediately after chest pain
dyspnea.character: inability to take deep breaths, air hunger

dry_cough.description: I keep getting this tickle that makes me want to cough but coughing makes the pain way worse

left_shoulder_pain.description: My left shoulder aches, it started around the same time as the chest pain

labs.CBC: WBC 8.1, Hgb 15.2, Plt 230 - within normal limits
labs.chest_xray: Moderate left-sided pneumothorax, approximately 30-35% collapse. Visible visceral pleural line. No mediastinal shift. No effusion. Right lung clear.
labs.ABG: pH 7.43, pCO2 36 mmHg, pO2 78 mmHg (mildly decreased pO2)
labs.ECG: Sinus tachycardia at 105 bpm, no ST changes, no axis deviation

surgical_history: Wisdom teeth extraction under general anesthesia (2024)
allergies: Seasonal pollen allergy (mild, no medications needed)

social_history.smoking: Vapes nicotine (JUUL) for about 2 years, roughly 1 pod every 2-3 days
social_history.alcohol: Drinks on weekends at college, 4-6 beers typically
social_history.drugs: Smoked marijuana a few times, last time about a month ago
social_history.living_situation: Lives in a dorm room with one roommate on campus
social_history.exercise: Plays pickup basketball 2-3 times per week, lifts weights occasionally
social_history.diet: Typical college diet — dining hall food, ramen, fast food. Takes protein shakes.

conditions.paternal_uncle: Had a collapsed lung in his twenties
conditions.grandparents: All living, no significant pulmonary history

personality: a little scared but tries to play it cool, uses humor to cope with anxiety, respectful but fidgety and restless, asks 'am I going to be okay?' multiple times
communication_style: Uses casual young-adult language ('bro it just popped', 'this is insane'). Answers questions directly but may need to be asked about vaping specifically — doesn't consider it 'smoking.' Will downplay alcohol and marijuana use unless asked in a nonjudgmental way. Texts friends updates from the ER between questions.
pain_description_style: Dramatic but genuine — 'dude this is like an 8, easily the worst pain I've ever felt.' Uses comparisons ('it feels like my lung deflated like a balloon'). Quick to give a number on pain scale. Winces visibly when taking deeper breaths.

critical_findings: Moderate (30-35%) left-sided pneumothorax on chest X-ray
Classic primary spontaneous pneumothorax demographic: tall, thin, young male
Acute onset pleuritic chest pain with dyspnea and no trauma
Nicotine vaping history — risk factor for pneumothorax
No mediastinal shift (not tension pneumothorax currently)
Referred left shoulder pain (phrenic nerve irritation — classic finding)
differential_diagnoses: Tension pneumothorax (must rule out — no mediastinal shift currently)
recommended_interventions: Supplemental oxygen via non-rebreather mask (accelerates pneumothorax reabsorption)
Chest tube insertion (pigtail catheter or small-bore tube) for 30-35% pneumothorax with symptoms
Continuous pulse oximetry and cardiorespiratory monitoring
Serial chest X-rays to assess lung re-expansion
Pain management (IV ketorolac or morphine, avoid excessive sedation that may suppress respiratory drive)
Monitor for tension pneumothorax (tracheal deviation, hypotension, JVD)
Thoracic surgery consult if lung does not re-expand or for recurrence prevention discussion
Education about recurrence risk (~30% within 1-2 years) and when to seek emergency care
Avoid air travel and scuba diving until resolved
